aliases: Peking Duck with pancakes half set

english: Peking Duck with pancakes half set

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302051 represents Peking duck food product as a closest parent; no specific half-set portion code exists in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP (Roasted duck) is the closest defensible parent; no Peking duck-specific or half-set code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for Peking duck as a prepared dish food product.

preparation_method: served with spring onion and cucumber

serving.description: Half set (approximately half a roast duck with pancakes, hoisin sauce, spring onion, and cucumber)
serving.natural_language_portion: A half Peking duck set typically comprises approximately 500 g total, including roughly 300–350 g of sliced roast duck meat and skin plus accompaniments of thin wheat pancakes, hoisin sauce, spring onion, and cucumber.

nutrient_source.title: Duck, domesticated, meat and skin, cooked, roasted — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 05141)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are based on USDA SR Legacy entry for roasted duck (meat and skin), which is the closest approved-source match for the duck component of Peking duck. Peking duck is prepared with a maltose glaze and served with wheat pancakes, hoisin sauce, and vegetables; the full set macros are adapted to reflect the composite dish. The duck-meat-and-skin roasted values from USDA are used as the primary anchor for per-100g macros, with a modest upward adjustment to carbohydrate and sugar to account for the maltose glaze and pancake contribution typical of a Peking duck set. This is an estimated composite; confidence is medium. HPB Singapore Foodo and ICMR-NIN do not carry a Peking duck entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Peking duck as a composite dish. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate derived from the mixed-meal composition: the duck meat and skin component has negligible glycemic contribution (GI ~0 for protein/fat-dominant foods), while the wheat pancakes (thin, steamed) are the primary glycemic driver. Thin Chinese wheat pancakes (báo bǐng) are analogous to wheat flour tortillas or thin flatbreads, which have a GI of approximately 30–52 in the University of Sydney GI Database (e.g., wheat flour tortilla GI ~30, white bread GI ~75). A composite GI of 50 is used as a conservative mid-range estimate for the full set. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 42.5) / 100 = 21.3, based on 500 g serving with 8.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from hoisin sauce, soy-based condiments, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Peking duck set consumption due to its high sodium content (approximately 380 mg per 100 g, equating to ~1900 mg per 500 g serving), which represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced hoisin sauce and avoiding additional soy dipping sauces can help mitigate sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat pancakes and maltose glaze, combined with high fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Peking duck sets in moderation; the wheat pancakes and maltose glaze contribute a moderate glycemic load (~21 per half-set serving), while the high fat content (approximately 24.5 g per 100 g) may slow glucose absorption but also contributes to excess caloric intake; portion control and limiting pancake quantity are recommended strategies.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat and cholesterol from duck skin, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat Peking duck as an occasional food; the duck skin is rich in saturated fat (~6.8 g per 100 g) and cholesterol (~89 mg per 100 g), and the high sodium content from condiments further elevates cardiovascular risk; removing or minimising duck skin consumption and limiting hoisin sauce can reduce saturated fat and sodium load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peking Duck (Half Set) is a classic Beijing Chinese dish consisting of approximately half a whole roast duck, served with thin wheat pancakes (báo bǐng), hoisin sauce, sliced spring onion, and julienned cucumber. The duck is prepared through a traditional multi-step process: the bird is air-dried, coated with a maltose syrup glaze, and then roasted in a wood-fired or hung oven at high temperature until the skin becomes lacquered, crispy, and deep mahogany in colour. The meat beneath is tender and juicy, with a rich, savoury, slightly sweet flavour profile. Diners typically wrap slices of crispy skin and duck meat in the thin pancakes along with hoisin sauce, spring onion, and cucumber. A half set is a common restaurant portion in Singapore and is suitable for two to three diners as part of a shared Chinese meal. Nutritionally, Peking duck is energy-dense (~337 kcal per 100 g), high in fat (particularly from the skin), moderate in protein (~19 g per 100 g), and contains a moderate carbohydrate contribution from the pancakes and glaze. The sodium content is elevated due to hoisin sauce and seasoning. Clinically, it is a high-concern food for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and saturated fat content, and a moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to the glycemic contribution of the pancakes and maltose glaze. It is not suitable for halal diets unless certified, and contains gluten from the wheat pancakes.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Peking duck half set?
How much sodium is in Peking duck?
Is Peking duck high in fat?
What is the GI of Peking duck?
What comes in a Peking duck half set?

confidence_reason: No exact Peking duck entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy roasted duck (meat and skin) with estimated adjustments for the composite dish including pancakes, hoisin sauce, and maltose glaze. GI is an analogous estimate based on the wheat pancake component from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the USDA duck roasted values are a strong anchor for the primary component, and the composite adjustments are conservative and well-documented.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated for the composite Peking duck set dish using USDA SR Legacy roasted duck (meat and skin) as the primary anchor, with upward adjustments to carbohydrate (~8.5 g/100g) and sugar (~3.2 g/100g) to account for the maltose glaze and wheat pancake contribution. 2. Sodium (~380 mg/100g) reflects the combined contribution of duck seasoning and hoisin sauce; actual restaurant sodium may vary significantly. 3. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Peking duck as a composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the access date. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for roasted duck used as the primary nutrient anchor. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy roasted duck values and are reported as best-available approximations. 6. The half-set portion (500 g) is an estimate based on typical restaurant serving sizes in Singapore; actual weight varies by restaurant. 7. Halal status is marked false as standard Peking duck preparation uses pork-derived hoisin sauce in many recipes; halal-certified versions exist but cannot be assumed. 8. The food is served at See Ya Tonight 今晚有局 (Singapore) per the source field survey record; price SGD 75.40 per half set.
